Okay. Hi. Welcome to another live webinar in halbertology. This week we have again Kevin Halbert on his weekly base trilogy presentation series. This week he's putting it all together in an aptly named presentation called Stringing It All Together. Yeah, so we're just going to string it all together. This isn't an end all be all. It's basically getting to that outline part and knowing what each component is likely or should be or is commonly in a sales letter and then how that component is supposed to be thought of or what basically things you have to remember when you're talking to that component say a call to action or a PS. Actually, I left the PS out. And I knew I was going to forget a few things but we have a whole bunch of people here to help me remember, hey, you forgot the PS which is supposed to reiterate the offer or drive people back up into the copy. So we're going to just cover a bunch of these pieces. They're just some major points that I don't know we would cover anywhere else that just gets to these when you do this in this component make sure to consider this or to do these pieces. So when it comes to starting off and checking, there's a bunch of things to put in the pre-flight that we can just talk about off hand that may not be in the text here. But the number one thing is, you know, we always got to know who the person is we're selling to and what they care about in that aspect. What is it that, you know, we've talked about selling computer monitors and there's a whole bunch of different reasons why a person might want a computer monitor if they have four screens going like I do that's a 4x trader versus a gamer or something like that. And you have to talk to what it is that they're doing. So we want to know what their prospects, their motion is. And here's another thing that's very important is to know a couple things. What is it you want that person to do after they've read your story? They come into the story from a certain place that's where they're not even thinking about you and you get in front of them and you distract their day. Okay, you sometimes make them shake their head and go, huh, what's that? You know, and stuff like that. And you're taking them and I forget what people call it, pattern interrupt. But really, yeah, it's a kind of a slap in the face to get you, hey, think about this instead. I'm going to focus on this. And so, but the thing is, is that none of this stuff matters until you get to the end and go, what does that want the person to do when they're done reading this? And that means how is their, how is this experience influenced them or changed their mind in some way to look at something to bring them to the way of thinking that you wanted to be at when they get the end of this ride, when they get to the end of the story? What was it? It's a wonderful life or something like that where the guy thinks the whole world would be better off if he was dead. And they do this whole illustration of what it would be like if he was not there. And so what it does is the whole story transforms the guy into a different perspective of when he gets dumped out the other end of that story and he looks at the world differently. And so that's a lot of what you want to do in a lot of these instances. You just want to excite people more or enrage them more or do something else. So it's very important to figure out what you want that reader to do when they're done, but also what frame of mind you have to put them in and what path cooking the frog slowly. You know, I think it might be a myth, but it might be true that when you put a frog in a pot of water, if you turn up the heat slowly enough, it will die because of the slow transition. It's so slow that it never gets this like shot to jump out of the pan. So yeah, you want to know what is the seduction path that takes them from where their frame of mind is down to where your frame of mind is or the frame of mind you want them to be when they get there. So that's very, very, very important. But to get there is to know what their dominant resident emotion is. What is their mindset? What is their perspective on that subject right now? Do they oblivious? They don't care. They feel the opposite of that. They already feel strong emotions, stuff like that. So you need to know where they're at mentally and emotionally to get to that point. So those are things you need to know before you start anything as far as a copy. But the Hemingway challenge, which the Hemingway challenge was to read an entire story in just six words. And the six words that he finally came up with, and now there's a bunch of them out there. And what they've done is they've gone and Hemingway challenged all these novels that were already written. And in fact, there's this funny thing that does movies in, was it five seconds? I think it's movies in five seconds or something. It's like all these funny YouTube videos that tell the whole story in like five seconds. But the Hemingway challenge was to be lean and efficient and to the point of what was exactly you're saying. And you can expand upon that. Once you condense it down, you can really expand it out, which was Hemingway's was four sale baby shoes never worn. And from that, you get a whole story. And it is another person talked about the short story ever written. A man was alone in the world in a room, and then there was a knock at the door. But so there are some short stories. My dad's favorite is obviously amazing and desperate and things like that. These are words that aren't just, you know, they're just, they're seriously powerful adjectives that speak to him that are more powerful than saying something. Now, what I suggest and tell everybody to do is instead of saying, hey, you know, Kevin, can I have your list of power words, you're going to, you know, that, you know, it's like you're trying to mimic Kevin. And I think if you get John's list of, John Carlton's list of power words, you're starting to sound like Stephen King, you know, I just want to, you know, murder your competition and get them by the jugular and so forth. But if you grab the ones that attract to you, then these are the words that you will more easily use. And so what I will do is create a list of power words that you, that you peruse just before, you know, just because they're floating around in your head before you actually sit down to do the copy dump or especially in the editing process. Don't try and get, you know, I mean, when you start, we're going to talk about getting your copy flow going once you do that, do you don't want anything to stop it? Don't let ugly writing stop it. I'm 35 years old. I'm a housewife and mother of three children. I'd like to tell you something first about myself. One day last summer, I just broke down and started to cry. So we got the who are you and we're setting the hook deeper. And what we, but what this is the thing. Who are you? Why should I listen to you? You've got to do that. And the thing is, it's to lend yourself authority is the reason your authority can deliver them to a benefit. Okay. And I said this, you have to do all of this fast. I'm, I'm really, I'm going to this kind of fast because I have a second slide that I really want to get to the point that I really want to make. Which is that, okay, I was sitting, I was sitting at lunch with Gary Benzavenga. And we were talking and we're sitting around the small table. And maybe I told you guys a little story. I'm going to so funny because the audience, when he went to tell a secret, everybody leaned in there. When I went to tell him a secret, everybody leaned in. It was just, there was an old ad was an EF Hutton talks. And then the person goes, my broker is EF Hutton and they're in this whole dining room restaurant and the whole place gets quiet and everybody leans towards the guy. And the slogan was when you have talks, everybody listens. It's very old, probably 30 year old, you know, or something. But I will always remember that they ran it. And it says something. But the thing was is that when Gary Benzavenga was telling me, he was saying, the biggest mistake I see everyone make as a copywriter is, they don't continue. They don't pay off the headline. Okay. A lot of people, you know, here's the headline and they go, you know, back in the thing when I was just starting, you know, they don't continue the exact thought. Well, almost like the second sentence of the headline is the first part of the first paragraph. So keeping that story going is, you know, you have to do that. And you can see that, you know, Desperate Housewife has come in writing to those first two or three cents of the debt copy. Okay. And so maybe I had two slides going on at once. Okay. So I just want to make sure that you understand that. You know, I guess, you know, there was a trick that somebody did, which I think is a kind of a good philosophy. They were fitting like a lot of copy into some old print advertising. And what they did was, is they start off with a headline. And then when they got into the text of the copy, the font size kept getting smaller and smaller because they knew the more they sucked the person in, the less the font size mattered. And I think, you know, I think in a lot of instances, that's what you're looking at. You're looking at the headline going down into the funnel all the way in the story. Not like, here's a chunk of a headline. Here's a chunk of this. Here's a chunk of that. But it's all one, basically one paragraph leading to an end. Okay. And so I think, you know, who's going to argue with Gary Benz, a Vengas device, but I think that's the whole point is that that headline, and everybody says the object of the headline is to get them to read the next sentence. And the object of that sentence is to get to the read the next sentence. Now, I think there's a little bit, you have to be a little lenient on the idea that sometimes you will get a little jerky on switching gears, but you try to make it as smooth as a grease shoot as possible for someone to read through that copy. First, when it, um, when it comes to the language, I want you to, you know, look at their terms, just like you're looking at your power words before you write, but again, don't let anything interrupt that copy flow. Because when you tone down the language and you tone down the vocabulary, you do that for, you focus on doing that in the editing phase. And you want to use an app called Hemingway. Um, and you put it through Hemingway and it'll tell you now, um, my, our father, um, or what actually first, what happened was one time I was taking, I was taking my son to a soccer practice and we needed to do an email and a campaign that would sell, um, of course. And so I had this idea for a hook in my head and I sat down and I started writing it out and everything like that. And I sent it out and it did quite well. Um, but Scott Haynes had called me and he said, you know, I was amazed by what you wrote and I said, why? And he said, because it, you know, I looked at it, I put it through the Hemingway app and I don't use the Hemingway app. He said it came into the three and a half grade level. And I was very impressed by that because, you know, I didn't use the Hemingway app to, to identify these notes and pull it down, but that's how simple we're talking about speaking here. So you can't be too basic. And if you want proof of that, the guy who beat me, somebody had put, put somebody put one of dad's either newsletters or one of his, uh, one of the web pages up and it was Harlan who did it. And he put it through the Hemingway app and it came at three third grade level. Now you're not trying to shoot and say, I'm going to get rid of this word that I need to use no matter what, um, because it doesn't make third level, third grade level. So like, for example, averted might not make third grade level. That's not your goal. Your goal is to actually just highlight the words that you can replace with a more simple word, as long as it's not any emotion evoking word that you're trying to use. Okay. And you do that. You're going to do that in the, in the editing phase. The other thing that I just wanted to talk about with that, the beginning, um, what I think you should do to just to get, make sure that you kick off and don't worry about the writer's block is understand one thing. And then I'll give you a little trick, which is your story is never really started at the first time, first sentence you write usually. And you sometimes you'll write or we'll write and then, you know, three, the first three paragraphs get killed, what could be the first five pages. And sometimes you're writing a very long sales piece. And then all of a sudden you get into this flow state and boom, the whole headline and the whole thing has to be redone because you found something better to start with something more powerful and emotional and evoking. Um, so, but one of the things that, so what I like to do to just get going, if, if you're like, okay, sitting there staring at a blank page. Is imagine somebody who's close to you that fits the most likely profile you have. So let's say it's a wedding. We're going to imagine you have a sister that's getting ready for the wedding. And you just say, you know, Hey, dear sis, I, you know, I know it's been a while since I spoke to you and I know you're about to get married. Here's something that I just thought you might like to know. Here's what's up. And you just start relaying the story. So, you know, there's a wedding singer or a wedding planner and she's done this and that you go into the story. You know, she was at this, uh, or, you know, I was at a fence place and there's the wedding all hell broke loose. Um, or at least we thought it was about to, you know, the brides and everybody's about to fight and everybody, the groom was nervous and all of these other things. But thankfully the wedding student, the wedding planner and some really sniffy things I'm going to tell you about crisis was avert, crisis was averted. But the point is what the trick is, if you started off like a letter, you could just, you're going to hack off that. You're never going to start with deer cysts in the beginning of an ad or an advertisement, but it's a good trick just to start getting the words out flowing. And sometimes I'll do that. And like I said, I'll cut off a whole page or a half a page to a page and a half just because that's where we got to all hell broke loose, which is a better way to start off. And you say, you know, I was at, I was at somebody's wedding. It was up in Santa Barbara. Um, you know, it was supposed to be on the beach and so forth. And the bride, bride looked beautiful, but then all hell broke loose. And you might just cut off all the beginning and say, the bride looks beautiful, but all hell broke loose. And then now, because even when you're telling a story, uh, in person or when, in so forth, there's that point where, okay, now you've got their attention. And if you can tell that story in person and see that look in their eye, or if you can read that and know this is where I would be all of a sudden paying attention, you're going to kind of lock off all the stuff that's in the beginning. But that's a pretty good way to just get the ink flowing out of your pen or just get those, the, the, the text and the letters put on a thing. And it may not work for you. It may just be something that works pretty well for me, but, um, I, you know, I think it's a, it's a great way to do it because it's, you get personal and you're just now relaying the hook and the story, the hook and the offer. And one last thing I want to say is at the very beginning, usually most of the ads start with either who, here's who I am, or they set the hook or, or, you know, a loop that they will close later, but they start the open loop. Um, the, the, the, there's, there's a, there's a phrase that makes, um, David and Christine, I guess laugh hysterically when I talk about features versus benefits in that, um, a feature is what it is. It's width, it's height, it's, um, length, it's color, it's whatever. And, um, and the benefits of what it does for you, how is your life changed by this feature being available? And the way that I always remember it, because it's funny to remember is that, uh, when it comes to Viagra, they're not buying the little blue pill. They're buying the boner. Okay. So if you keep that in mind, people will always remember the difference between features and benefits. You guys are all cracking up now. So yes. I can't believe we let you get boner in something again. You just sneak it in. Sorry. Yeah. I just, you know, I had, I had a bet with my brother that he said, I bet you can't get away with using the word boner in a webinar. No, I'm just kidding. So, but the little blue pill, blue pill, it's just, it's kind of a, what do we call those when you use the same first letter, um, whatever those things are. Alliteration. So any case, but the point being is if you remember that people don't care. That's a little blue pill. They wouldn't care if it was a large yellow liquid. They want to buy the benefit. So keep that in mind when it comes to the benefits that you're selling. And I think that you need to know about features. Is that features are a way to make the benefits believable. Okay. So right as an example, I said this V8 engine takes up 421 horsepower for a heart pumping experience that will make all your friends cars shrink in your room. Okay. One thing I want to point out is these, a lot of these subheads in this particular one, um, you know, if you, you could pull those out and you could read this copy without skipping a beat and you wouldn't, you wouldn't feel like you were missing, you wouldn't know that you were missing the headlines or the subheads. And so one of the things about them is, um, you know, he, if you scroll down a little bit to the first subhead on the left scroll, um, when you read them from the, from the paragraph right before the subhead and then right after one, let me tell you why I think my diet secret is so fantastic. I mean, I'm not saying you don't need it. You want the sub. What I'm saying is, um, the, the copy flows with or without them, but the beautiful thing about it is, let's suppose you're attracted by melts fast, fat fast. And you go right in. First of all, this diet works fast. You can jump into the copy right there, continue reading the ad without going back up to the top. And that's the test of a good subhead. A good subhead draws the attention of the reader from who's not who's skimming it in good subhead, uh, could flow right through it with, you know, almost as if that, you know, the top paragraph from the bottom paragraph, it can, or can even lead up to an emphatic, exciting part of the story that becomes the subhead. And then the next part is continuation. So it's something where it reads well from the, all from the top through it. It reads well from the subhead right down into the chute slide. And it would practically even read well without it. And, um, you know, that, and so when after you write the headline, you know, you might be editing it to, uh, to, to make sure that it passes that test. Cause that's, that's very important test. You know, I don't know if you guys ever experience every, of course you have. Whenever you accidentally look at the sun, you close your eyes and all of a sudden everywhere you look, you see the sun. Do you know, it kind of gets burned on your retina for a couple of seconds. This is the kind of subheads that you could glance at. It will burn in your head. You do not have to read it. Do you know what I mean? It comes into your head. You know, the other things are a sentence. You have to process that sequence that conceptualize it. All this stuff is like you looked at the sun and you could just glance at it and it will stay in your head. Okay. Okay. So, uh, moving on to slide, uh, I'm going to like 14 or 15 or 21. Um, and, uh, and we see that it's quoted and look at, we got the ellipses going and italicized and there's something that they said psychologically because of, you know, it's like a Pavlovian or a conditional thing because usually times are quoted all the time, not in marketing, but in articles and other stuff. It's almost mentally just automatically registered as a fact when you do a quote. Okay. That's why these are italicized and that's why they're in quotes. And a lot of times why you want to write them in a, um, um, which you don't see here, but a lot of times you'll see some headlines written in a newsy way. You know, newspapers like to write the biggest headline possible, but they use all these abbreviations to, to make the sentence work. Um, but anything that leads it to, to, uh, um, people talk, it will get into some of the language of difference from up when other people are talking. Um, so this is, um, we got a quote in the italicized text. We're using the ellipses. We show the white space, lots of breathing room and it peels to the strongest interest of the reader. Okay. You will lose faith, wait faster on this diet than if you ran seven miles every day, you will be able to measure the difference, your waistline in 24 to 36 hours. I think this is the fastest, safest diet in the world. If you can find a diet that works faster, I will buy it from you and glad you pay your money for it. Okay. Um, so what we have here is kind of these, these Hemingway shortest. It's like we have these different tiers of how, how, how detailed we're going to get on our story. If they can only take it in the signings amount, that's what we'll give them. If they'll commit a little bit more, we'll give them more. If they want bigger, we'll give them the whole page. Okay. If you guys have any comments, that's fine. So I'll move on to the next part, which will be, go ahead. Uh, I just want to add when it comes to breaking up the paragraphs, cause that's what we've already kind of covered. I want to add this into it. Um, you want the paragraphs to be short. And so there'll be easy spots to say, okay, this, you know, we can break these two paragraphs, but do not break them and think about it in the school way, you know, where it has to, you know, the, the idea of paragraphs is that you lumped a few sentences together that made a complete thought. That's not your goal here. Sometimes I will break up those sentences in the one, and I want to break it. You know, there are two sentences, but I want one sentence set, set apart, especially towards the beginning of the copy, because I want to make somebody feel incomplete without reading the next sentence. And so when you break it, sometimes I like to end the set. I will even rephrase the sentence so that I can break it. And so I can, when I break it off, I leave it on this high note that lets like a, it's kind of like a joke, you know, the jumps always got to have the punch line at the end. You can never stick it right near the end. It has to be the end. And, uh, you can tell jokes on Facebook and see how that works out. It's funny, but the, um, I will, you know, add some punch, rearrange the sentence to the punches at the end. But when I break up a paragraph, I want them to, um, you know, it's not always along the lines that you would with a, uh, you know, if you're writing a school document or you're trying to, you know, write a novel where you're trying to express a complete thought always. And, you know, so, and abbreviate as much as you can. I mean, you know, you, when, when you go through the editing process, every word's got to earn its place. So, um, risk reversal is, um, this is what you have to do. You have to be, when you're done with your risk reversal, you have to say, and they get the reader to say in their mind, I really do have nothing to lose by trying this. I'll say what you want to say. Well, I just got two things to add. Um, first, uh, when it comes to the clothes and, you know, what you're doing with risk reversal, you know, you amp it up the more of that, that decision is a hard one to make. So that could be based on price. It could be an experience of the buyer. You know, it's a lot easier for you to go and pick up a DVD for $20 than it is to make the decision to purchase a home. That's an exaggeration, you know, long exaggeration. Um, so I'm going to give you Gary Halbert's killer clothes for really high ticket items. Um, just, okay, let's suppose you're selling a forex system and it's a, it's a higher decision to make it because somebody's not, they're a stock trader, let's say, and they're, they're not into forex that you're going after as a target. You know, it might not be, maybe you just go and be going after a forex trader. And the newer they are and the more expensive the course is, the more it's going to be harder for them to make that decision. So what you want to do is do a, what if I'm wrong? What if I'm right analysis? And I've seen my father actually write this and say, and I wrote this actually in a piece, not too long ago and say, you know, what if I'm, I'm going to, you know, but what if I'm wrong and what, but what if I'm wrong? You know, and I really don't, you know, live in the city. I don't, you know, my, I'm lying about my wife and children and I'm going to take your money and go, we'll run off to Costa Rica and live the rest of my life on your $3,000 or whatever it is. And it then takes you a, you know, 90 days to get your money back through your, through your credit card company or something. You know, you will have been out some time and some energy and at the most a little bit of money, but what if I'm right? What if I'm right? And what I've given you is a new way to make money for the rest of your life. And what if I'm right? And you now have the freedom that I was talking about. And it's a simple, easy transition to go from trading stocks to trading the forex and making even more money. You know, what if I, you know, what if this system, you know, is adding, you know, 3% a day to most traders' pockets and, you know, what, but what if, what if it only added 1% you know, you know, and, and I did that once also with a, with a subject line product where I was explaining, you know, what if I'm wrong and you've wasted 20 bucks, but what if I'm right? What if, what if this, what would it mean to you if I actually, this trick use increases your open rate just, you know, 1% for the rest of your life, you know, but, and here it is doing it for 10% and getting 30% opens total or increasing other people's. And so the harder that decision is, the more you go into that kind of an analogy. And it's just a great way to close. The other quick two things I want to mention is when you're doing, and we'll talk more about this, I guess, in editing, you know, when you read the copy and stuff like that, when you're going back in, you're emphasizing, you know, the one thing that we didn't cover because we are, you know, we're jumping around, it's some formatting is you have a list of things that can, that grab attention in copy, a list of format, which is all caps, bolds, underlining, italicized. Online, you have color and emotion, and motion actually, you know, but you don't want to use motion that's distracting constantly, unless it's something that's going to quickly be scrolled out of sight. You know, but there are all these different way and combinations of it. You will get more attention using all bolds, underlined and all caps. Okay, that's like screaming, you don't want to mess, but you don't want to overdo this and start screaming at your prospect because it really is. And you know, when you're telling a story and then you might emphasize a certain part that's really exciting, but you're, you know, but you don't want to emphasize every word like that because it's like screaming at your reader and nobody wants to be screamed at. And so just remember that it's a nice mixture and you don't want to overdo it, but when you'll go back through the copy, you'll underline and be more emphatic on different stuff. And it's an, it's easy to, you know, I mean, you can tell that being italicized is not nearly as traumatic as being bold and caps and so forth. And you can give just a natural feeling for it. And the last thing I want to add is the PS trip. The reason I want to talk about that is just because it belongs to the section. The headline, the sub, the sub heads are bullet points, you know, and the PS are 90% of your copy, 80% of the headline alone. That's why you spend so much time focusing on headlines. But the way that you're, you're going to track three different types of readers. You're going to track people who read the headline and go, that's it. I want to learn more. And they start reading right from the beginning and go through all the copy. And you have another type of reader that's going to see that headline and say, what's this about? And they're trying to make the decision if they've got the time or this is to them or if they want to read the copy and they'll scroll through it and the bullets and the PS will make them decide that, that they're going to read it. Okay. And there's the third kind of the people who actually read the headline. They read through the bullets and the, and the sub heads and they read the, the club, the PS and the clothes real quick and they buy. There actually are people who just do that alone. But the PS for it to be a good PS has to work for all three of those readers. And a lot of the times when you're in a flow state, you will come along with a fantastic PS that sums it all up, ties it all up, brings it emotion, you know, really, you know, drives desire, excuse me, and so forth. But, you know, when you're in a fix for it, what I like to do is it's a simple trick. When you're creating a campaign, you are almost always creating a sense of urgency. So with our wedding planner, the sense of urgency is going to be booking her before she's filled up. Okay. I'm just making the assumption. That's what we'll go. And so what you want to do is a simple trick when you just need to write a PS and like you have two minutes to do it and, you know, the deadline's coming in. You just want to write the word. Remember, repeat the benefit, not a very specific, but you want to repeat the benefit and then the certain sense of urgency that you've outlined. So you say, you know, I'll give you two examples with the wedding. Say, remember, you know, this wedding planner, this wedding planner for, you know, and this wedding planner will reduce, make sure that your wedding is absolutely stress-free or your money back, but she only has a few more spots left open for this, for this wedding season. So that will make the person who's reading and skimming it trying to decide whether or not they're going to get to read the ad, say, I've got to read the ad now, because if I don't, I could miss out because that's why the sense of urgency helps drive them to reading that ad. If they, but it's a nice tie in it and it's a nice wind up punch at the end of somebody who's already read from the beginning all the way through. And it tells the person who's a skimmer and only a skimmer that they better read that off for now because of that's again, that sense of urgency. This is not what you should always do for a PS. This is just something that you can do. If you're in a desperate need to get a PS out, you're on a deadline and so forth. It's a trick that I think, I call these things like cheat codes. They're kind of like going into a computer and you hit these codes and it's like, okay, that will do it for that thing. And where these kind of tricks are even more useful in emails because you don't want to spend two months crafting a great email if you're creating a series of emails and auto responder series. So one of the things you got to do when you're doing this is learn how to write faster and it's a trick that I've used quite effectively and I've done it with even the subject line thing. So listen, you remember, I can be, the subject line, this long-term trick is helping people increase rates 30% of the time, but there's only X number of copies that are left. And you just repeat your sense of urgency at the end of that. And it's a simple trick to finish off a PS. But the PS could also be just a really great piece of the copy near that was near the end that you summed it up. And what you really did was just trying to change the format. That's another way to do it. Which reminds me, I forgot to put in a bonus offer. So maybe we'll just tag that on next week or something. Yeah. We'll just clean up loose ends as we go. Yeah. So like I said, well, that's a good point. Let's suppose you're doing the flow and at the end and you're like, and for a final, I'm going to off make this bonus and you have no PS. You can actually just take that piece lower and put PS there. And bonus. Yeah, we'll get into bonuses because there's a lot to say about a bonus. Okay. Okay. David, Mark, do either of you have anything you'd like to chime in on with regard to stringing it all together? Um, one thing that, uh, you know, we touched on proof. Um, and you know, Gary, Vincent, Benga has been a big part of, of me becoming a copywriter and certainly becoming the copyrighter. I am. And, and he is absolutely, I mean, emphatic about proof. But one of the things that, you know, I see a lot of times, you know, people think that they can throw up all these examples and, and I may not face it. We show proof that should just absolutely get people to send us their money right now because, you know, it costs this much and look at how much is being made. But I think, I think there's levels there and, you know, proof that it works, proof that it can work for them, proof that, that it's something that they can do. And then I think you got to go one more step and you got to prove that this is something that actually works. It's something they can actually do. It can actually work for them. But also then you got to prove the benefits of that happening because any, they're, they're parting with money. They very well might be part of the time if they're buying a course or something where they've got to invest, not just their money, but their time to go through it. And so I think you have to go back through and prove the, the, the individual benefits and that it's going to give them the result that they actually want. You got to prove it's a result they actually want. I think there's just a lot of levels of proof that I think maybe we could, I mean, get into it a future, a future webinar, but I, you know, proof is, it's, it's not just one thing. It's not saying, look, see here, this worked for so and so. And I think that it's a lot, I think people are a lot more skeptical than they once were. And I think that, you know, sometimes we throw so much proof out there that I just scratched my head and say, why didn't everybody buy this? And it's because they don't believe it, but it's not that they're saying you're a liar, you made up all that proof as much as it's saying, sure, it worked for that person, but it's not going to work for me. So I'd like to see a little more on proof. I think you should help us present the proof. But one of the things I think that helped with proof as far as I know, I haven't had to work with that a lot, but is, is that ability to, what was it? It was a correct, Clemens. Was any time up proof? And there was a way to, it would pass the tab test, you know, they could say something and they could tab right into something. And I think if you, you know, if you hand them something that's a link into, you know, let's say even better, as you put in the, a poll quote from the other place, but there's a link that goes in and they're like, I see it here. And now I go to this third party and it's exactly what he said it was. You know what I mean? It was, it's sitting there staring at my face. And I think one of the things you can do in there is help them read that or help them interpret what that means sometimes. But I also think it's in the, I've also think it's in the story. You know, one of the things that, you know, one of the things I noticed with proof is almost always when you're telling the story, you want the, the protagonist to be worse off, start off in a worse situation. That's why it's the one legged golfer. Cause if that one legged golfer can do it, I got two legs. They must be really, really hit that ball, you know, and it's, uh, that was, you know, yeah, I think we can go into a whole lot of different ways that you can do proof when you're, when you're assembling all the, all the facts and facets that you want to put together. Yeah. We see a lot of, uh, fake proof with these, um, uh, checks and these login stats to their accounts, stuff like that. That's been a kind of a, you know, it gets proofs get harder. Um, but yeah, we could do a whole thing on it. Um, I'd like to see how everybody here shows proof. Um, I think it's worth, worth the attention. I think headlines alone is worth the attention. We, we might, we should get into heavy on that. Uh, Christine, I've just got some noise before we finish off and, um, I'm not particular order. Um, for the, the, there will be some people in the audience who are not professional copywriters and who don't want to be professional copywriters. And a lot of the conversation that we have and have had is centered around talking to people like us. But I think what we've got to remember is that a copy in some form is really a form of manipulation. That's a form of mimicking what, um, has worked in the past and honing those skills to try and polish something to the point where we can make people take an action, but nothing will beat if you're just a person trying to write, uh, an email or a newsletter or your own sales letter, nothing will beat your own enthusiasm because the objective of the copywriters try and find that enthusiasm or the hot buttons or the points that make things work. Uh, for headers, things that we have found, uh, we usually test three headers at a time, um, always test a graphic with a header. So if you're going to have, um, do a three-way split test, have your strongest, um, headline and then have some form of graphic decided behind it or around it and then have something totally different for the third one. Um, especially for downloads. So for pages with downloads, what we've found is that in our own tests, if you have a background, it improves downloads by over 150%. That's the minimum improvement that we've found just by adding backgrounds. Um, as far as the, you spoke a little bit about, um, you know, the rating at a certain grade level and I've put in some studies by a Harvard review that are very interesting about how men and women think and, and how, uh, and I'll get to that point or another point that they make. But the brain itself, what it does is when it's reading through anything, if it finds a word that it doesn't understand, it continues to read, but the brain stops, the brain stops to try and untangle that word or it tries to decipher that word. So the more complicated, unless you know, absolutely, you're writing to professors or a different type of grade level, but that's why the simpler that you can write the better. So the brain doesn't need to think and on the deck, um, you know, you want to try and put people as an episodic memory. You want to suspend belief because if you're going to take people from a position of being a co prospect to get another wallet, they have to put them in a position where they can believe what you're saying. Harvard review, one of the articles that are put in there says that there's two things going on when you write. There's the idea, there's the linguistic idea of the words that you're, the idea you're trying to communicate. And there's another level going on where people are trying to establish themselves with some kind of status in relationship to you. So when you're writing your newsletter, your email, people are trying to figure out who you are. Are you the boss? Are you the guru? Are you the friend? Are you the, or are you to them? So, um, and that's got a lot to do with proof as well. How successful proof is, is, is not how much there is over, but it's how well you communicate the idea of proof. Another interesting thing in one of those articles that are posted is that girls tend to focus on relationships when they're reading coffee and men trying to are constantly trying to figure out the status, uh, as far as a copy goes. And one of the things that I've discovered or, or moving towards is in the last five years particularly is what I call being naked. So when you write, uh, there used to be, if you owned a business five years ago, 10 years ago, there was a barrier between you and the client. You know, you didn't have to talk to them. You didn't have to have social media. You didn't have to have anything. Now, particularly with Generation X, they want to, um, be equal. They want to touch you. They want to know you. Uh, and a good example of that was Taylor Swift with the argument with Apple just recently. And who won? Basically, you know, she said to Apple, we don't agree with what you're doing with this new, uh, the royalties for the music, but we also acknowledge you have to have a business. So the, she kind of spoke for the whole industry and Apple listen and said, yeah, okay, you're right. We will do that. So when I say being naked, the, one of the things that a lot of copywriters miss is that, um, personality of themselves, those little things that people identify as, he's a real person. He screwed up. He does something dumb or he does something great and he shared it. So, and that's quite a hard skill actually for people to, uh, for a copyright or to, to write if it's contrived. And that's why, you know, if it comes from the heart, it's a lot easier, but it's very few. I've not seen it done successfully very often where somebody can step into a niche and sort of have that nakedness where they can, because they haven't experienced those emotions. Um, but otherwise, I think it was a great presentation, Kevin. Covered nearly all the points. Great. Okay. And Gary Halbert was great at all that stuff. I have a question for you, Mark. I have a question for you, Mark, and that was good stuff. Um, when you mentioned the background behind downloads, uh, what exactly kind of background are you talking about? Like, you know, the background, like a fish aquarium or just a textured background, you know, well, if you can imagine, and, uh, we could probably get an example of it, but what we just, for the hell of it, we normally, when we have a download, we'll take someone from a squeeze page to a download where they can download a PDF or a report. So, um, about two, three months ago, we decided to do a test where we would have a plain white background, a solid color background, and one with an image. And the image that we chose was the skyline, a deep blue skyline of the New York skyline, Manhattan skyline, actually, and it covered the top 30% behind the download button. And that one image, uh, was something like, I can't remember, 180, 190% more downloads by using that image. So we thought it was a bit of a flip. So we tested it a couple of times and each time we tested it, having the background improved, uh, the downloads. I'm not, I don't know why, but it just, it's just before or after the squeeze. After. Might be because it could be less boring. You know, it could be just less boring and easier to look at while the download was going. Yeah. I mean, that's the number one reason I stopped the download is boredom. No, but it's, it's, well, it's, I, Mark, you're one of the eternal testers.
